chunk_summary: Resource-limited milk banks can ensure safety by focusing on critical control points, including pasteurization, cold-chain management, and competency-based training, while maintaining traceability and quality assurance for donor human milk (DHM).
learning_objectives: Understand critical safety controls for milk banking in resource-limited settings.
Learn the importance of Holder pasteurization and its time-temperature verification.
Identify strategies for cold-chain management and power resilience in hot climates.
Recognize the role of competency-based training and auditing in reducing contamination risks.
key_facts: Holder pasteurization requires a verified time-temperature profile using calibrated thermometers.
Pooling should only involve donors with complete screening documentation and aseptic technique.
Cold-chain failures are common in hot climates; facilities should maintain freezer logs, alarms, and backup power systems.
Transport of DHM requires validated insulated boxes with frozen gel packs and discard rules for thawed products.
Quarantine space must hold pasteurized DHM until microbiology results confirm safety.
Low-cost audit tools like daily checklists and batch record scoring can detect safety issues early.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Holder pasteurization can be delivered using a dedicated pasteurizer or a temperature-controlled water bath, but the time–temperature profile must be verified with calibrated thermometers and documented for every batch.
document_key_topics: Safety controls in resource-limited milk banks
